narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.60—which is below the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 30, lower than the usual 36.70. The admissions model predicted an 83.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, a risk level high enough to lead to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass and succeed in practice. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged most strongly on your academic decile rankings from earlier years in school. Your Year 3 decile of 1 was the biggest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure, as it signals consistently lower performance compared to most peers who rank much higher on average around the 5th or 6th decile. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile, also a 1—well below the typical 5.7 average—which reinforced concerns about foundational academic struggles that often predict bar exam challenges. Interestingly, your upper-middle family income had a mild positive sway, nudging the prediction slightly toward success since students from similar backgrounds tend to have better outcomes than average, but it wasn't strong enough to offset the academic red flags. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your lower deciles and test scores place you in a tougher spot, where the model sees patterns of underperformance that have tripped up others before. While full-time status is common and neutral here, it didn't help balance things out. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many from similar circumstances thrive with targeted effort—but the model focuses on these key indicators from past data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergrad record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and seeking mentorship from Black law alumni who navigated similar paths to bar success. Building a clearer track record of academic improvement could flip the script on automated reviews like this one. With persistence, you're positioning yourself for stronger opportunities ahead.
